The affairs of the Hospital are managed by
a Committee of 13 members, called in the
Act of Parliament *“ The Committee of
Management.” This Committee is elected
by the Governors from among their own
number, at their Annual General Meet-
ing, heI-::'l on the second Monday in
]?.nuary In each year. No one but a
Governor is eligible to be elected on this
Committee—11th George IV., chap. 72.

ADMISSION OF PATIENTS

Govenors or Subscribers wishing to have
a patient admitted to the house should give
him or her, as the case may be, the

-necessary order of admission to the Resident

Medical Officer, Barrington’s Hospital, and
if there is a bed vacant, and the case is a

suitable one for treatment, the patient will
be received. |

9

Orders of admission must be made on the

printed form, which may be had on applica-
tion to the Registrar.

Urgent cases are taken in at all hours of
the day or night, without an order of
admission.

_The Dispensary is open all day and
night for the treatment of accidents.

NoTE—As the funds at the disposal of the
Governors are not large, and as the Institu-
tion was founded principally for the
treatment of accident cases,it is to be hoped
that the subscribers will recommend as
few Medical cases as they possibly can.

LAND VALUES DUTIES.

(I.) Increment Value Duty.—Payable on
the occasion (a) of any transfer or sale of
land or any interest therein, (b) of anwy
lease for more than 14 years, (e) of the
land, or interest in it, passing on death.
(d) In the case of Corporations, in addition
to (a) and (b), and in place of (c), the
duty is payable in 19214, and every 15 years
thereafter.

Rate of Duty (payable as a stamp duty
by (a) the seller. (b) the lessor, (¢) the de-
ceased’s estate, or (d) the Corporation).—£1
for every £5b of “increment value,” i.e., the
increase in the wvalue of the site—apart
from the value of buildings, &e., thereon—
since April 30, 1909, or since the last pay-
ment of duty.

Exemptiions, &c.—Agricultural land while
it has no higher value than for agricul-
tural purposes only.—Small residences

occupied and cultivated by the owner, and
not. exceeding 50 acres (of average value

the subject of a mining lease or were
being worked on April 30, 1909.—Minerals
not so exempt are subject to a special
bagis of charge to Increment Value Duty,
a3 an annual duty.

(IX1.) Reversion Duty—payable by the

Rate of Duty.—£1 for every £10 of the

Exemptions, &c.—Reversions purchased

Leases of agricultural land.—Leases the

crement Value Duty has been paid for the,

game benefit or increment.—Mining leases

(III&. Undeveicped Land Duty.—Payable
by the owner (including a lessee for a

which has not been developed by the erec-

greenhouses as trade buildings), or is not
otherwise used bona-fide for any trade,
ete., other than agriculture.

Rate of Duty—One halfpenny annually
for every £1 of the “site wvalue,” i.e., the
market value of the fee simple of the land
if divested of buildings, timber, &ec., and
lese the value of any minerals.

Exemptions; &c.—Land the site value of
which does not exceed £50 an acre.—Agri-
cultural land, except on such part of the
gite value as exceeds its agricultural value.
—Parks and spaces open to the public as
of right, or to which the public are allowed

uged as such under agreements for not

Garden (with & dwelling-house) up to 5

ment terminates.—Agricultural land occu-

COPYRIGHT.

Pamphlets, Lists, or Books for Advertis-
ing Businesses, original or of literary
value (i.e, not merely a string of names),
are copyright, and may be protected for a
serm of years for the author’s or the pro-
rietor’s sole use. The copyright runs
rom the date of the first publication and
lasts for 42 years, or for the life of the
author and seven years after, whichever
be the longer term. The rights vest in the
author or person at whose expense the
literary labours were carried out. It isnot
compulsory to register the copyright, but
when this is done the productions may be
marked “Entered at Stationers’ Hall,” and
after the work hag been registered applica—
tion may be made to the courts for an
injunction and for damages against those
who have made free with this particular
form of one's property. A registration fee
of 53. establishes the right to sue for in-
fringement and for a similar fee the
author can assign his right to another.
A trade label can in the same way be
protected from piracy by a fee of 1Is.
Registration effected with the Registrar,
%t.g-tiouera’ Hall, Ludgate Hill, London,

Artistic Copyright.—A poster, advertiging
tablet, enamel, or linen sign may all be
subject to the Fine Arts Copyright Act. It
is essential that the picture, drawing, or
photograph be original, and that {he
author (or holder for a good considera-
tion) be a British subject, or resident in
the British dominions. Registration is not
compulsory, but is necessary if active
steps are to be taken for the protection of
one’s rights. Artistic Copyright runs from
the time the picture is made, and lasts for
the life of the author (or owner of rights),
and for seven years after. Registration is
effected at Stationers’ Hall, as above, and
iz secured by deposit of one photograph or
copy and the filing of the name and ad-
dress of the author and payment of above
fee. Persone buying original pictures for
the purpose of advertising should see that
the copyright is expressly reserved to
them by agreement in writing. Failing
any agreement, the copyright ceases to
exist.

In most circumstances Book Copyright
or Artistic Copyright covers all reguire-
ments, but orne who wants to be doubly
safe should resort to the further registra-
tion at the Patent Office of some particu-
larly cherishable picture, design or mark.
The United States has stood outside the
Copyright convention, and those who wish
to safeguard American rights have to com-
ply with certain obligations as to printing
in America.

merce, and to pattern, ghape, configura-

WILLS.

Where property is considerable, and of
different kinds, or even where inconsider-
able, if of different kinds, and to be dis-
posed of to married or other persons, or for
the benefit of children, for charities, or
trusts of any description, it is absolutely
necessary and proper that a gualified legal
adviser should superintend the execution
of the will

Other forms of wills give particular lega-
cies to adults, or to infants, with direction
for application of interest during minority;
to infants, to be paid at twenty-one with-
out interest; specific legacies of govern-
ent stock; general legacies of ditto; specific
legacies of leasehold property; immediate
or deferred annuities; to daughters or sons
for life, and after them their children;
legacies with directions for the applica-
tion of the money; bequests to wife, with
conditions as to future marriage; define
the powers of trustees, provide for and
direct the payment of debts, &c. All these
more complicated forms of wills require
the superintendence of a professional ad-
viser.

Covernment Trusiee for Wills.
The Government in the person of ite

‘ officer, “The Public Trustee,” will under-

take the trusteeship of any will or settle-
ment if appointed or desired. The Public
Trustee will not act if the estate be thought

| to be insolvent or if the duties would in-

volve the carrying on of a business, nor

' will he act in any trust created for phil-

anthropic or religious purposes. Though
the Public Trustee may for sufficient reasons
decline to act, he is in_no case to refuse
his services merely on the ground that the
estate is of small value. The responsibility
for the safety of the trust funds will fall
upon the Government itself.

National Insurance. 45

NATIONAL

(Bill passed the House of Commons
December 6th, 1911, as applied to Ireland).

Provides for a Board of Irish Insurance
Commissioners, with central office in Dublin.

Commissioners—J. A. Glynn, Chairman;
Walter J. Kinnear, Dr. W. Joseph Maguire,
Mrs. Dickie.

All contributions, and all sums paid out
of moneys provided by Parliament in re-
spect of benefits, and expenses of admini-
stration of such benefits, are paid into a
fund called the Irish National Health Fund,
under the control of the Irish Insurance
Uommisgioners; and the sums required to
meet expenditure properly incurred, by
Approved Societies and Local Health Com-
mittees, for the purpose of such benefits
and their administration, are paid out of
the fund.

Local Health Committees, to consist of
24 members, appointed by regulations of
the Irish Insurance Commisgioners so as
to secure representation of the insured
persons resident in the County or the
County Borough, two at least to be medical
practitioners apd two women.

The duties of the Local Health Com-
mittee is to administer benefits for all in-
gured persons in societies and post office
deposit ingurance, and to report and make
recommendations concerning the public
health in their areas, &c.

Approved Societies to administer the
benefits may be friendly societies of any
description, trade unions, or other organ-
izations which satisfy certain conditions,
grouping themselves in County and County
orough areas, and establishing a central
fund under the management of the Local
Health Committee. The contributions of
persons compulsorily insured, not belong-
ing to an approved society, and those of
the employer and State, will be credited
to them in a post office deposit fund. Every
person insured must either become a
member of an approved society or a post
office deposit contributor.

County Councils may at any time before
the expiration of one year from the com-
mencement of the Act, submit to the Irish
Insurance Commissioners a scheme for the

establishment of 'a County RBociety, to insure’

persons resident in the County not being
memhers of any other Approved Society at
reduced rates of benefit.

Medical Benefits.—An insured personin
Ireland is not entitled to medical benefits
gave as an additional benefit in an approved
society, when it shall be administered by
the Local Health Committee, unless the
Irish Insurance Commissioners otherwise
direct. The joint contributions of the in-
gured and employer in Ireland is 5id in-
stead of 7d as in England, where medical
benefits are included.

Whe are Insured.—Every employed per-
gon, male or female, not more than 65
vears of age, over 16, whether paid by time
or piece or otherwise, will be compulsorily
insured, except:—

(1) Non-manual workers (e.g., clerks, earn-
ing more than £160 a year).

(%} Commigsion agents working for more
than one employer.

(3) Jobbing gardeners, washerwomen,
gempstresses, &c., working on their own
account.

(4) Wives employed by husbands, and
children working for parents and pot in
receipt of wages.

(5) Clasual domestic employees and casual
labourers not employed for business pur-
poses, e.g., luggage carriers.

Telegraphs.

The charge is 6d for the first twelve
words. including the address, and 3d. for
every additional word. Counting—compoung
words, proper names, figures, abbrevia-
tions, &c.

The cost of registering an abbreviated
address, which must consist of two words,
18 £1 1s.

Telegraph offices are usually open on
weeli-days from 8 a.m. to 8 p.m., but in
London and most of the largest provinecial
townsg, offices are always open, day and
night, and in many towns telegrams may
be despatched at any hour from the rail-
way station.

Telegrams in plain language in any
modern European tongue are charged for
according to the number of words; but all
words not forming part of any modern
European language, and all unintelligible
combinations of letters are charged for at
the rate of five letters to a word.

Exceptions—Words which are ordinarily
written as one, or coupled by hyphens, ags
“mother-in-law,” “forty-seven,” “warehouse-
man,” are connted as one word.

Such names as O'Neill, MacDonnell, and
names with the prefix ‘‘St.” are charged
for as single words, but double names like
Bouverie-Tracy, although written with a
hyphen, count as two words. Initial lettera
are each counted as one word.
~ Buch abbreviations as “can’t,” “won’t,”
“couldn't’” are counted as single words.

Figures are counted at the rate of five
figures to a word. For example, ‘38563
eounts as one word, while 385634 counts
as two words. Names of towns and vil-
lages in the United Kingdom count as one
word. When words are underlined, or placed
in a parenthesis, or within inverted commag,
one extra word is charged for.

One word each is also charged for the
ordinary signs of punctuation if they are
required to be transmitted.

When the address is beyond the limit of
free delivery, porterage is charged at the
rate of 3d per mile, or part of a mile, the
distance being calculated from the limit
of free delivery.

.Telegrams (Forelgn and Coloniai).

Must be written on special forms and
are accepted in the language of any of
the prineipal FEuropean countries. The
rates vary. See Official Postal Guide.

with Ships at Sea.

Office for transmission to certain of the
principal ocean liners by wireless tele-
ﬁ-raphy from the stations at Caister, Orook-
1aven, The Lizard, Malin Head, Niton, the
North Foreland, and Rosslare, at a charge
of 63d a word, with a minimum of 6/6 per
telegram, and telegrams from ships are
received at these stations and thence trans-
mitted by wire.

Messages are also accepted at any tele.
graph office for transmission to His Ma-
Jesty’'s ships from the same stations at a
charge of 33d a word, with a minimum of
3/6 per telegram. Such telegrams are only
accepted at sender’s rigk.

gether with information as to charges,

From the ** Leader,”” SMay 20th, 1911.

* Our readers will have a general recollection of the little controversies that took place some
time back in our columns on the question of Irish Christmas cards. The only firm last year, so
far as we know, that advertised Irish Christmas cards was the firm of Messrs, Guy & Co.

- Ltd., of Cork. The shortage in lrish Christmas cards last year was very much commented
upon. Well, we are glad to see that Messrs. Guy & Co., of Cork, are alive to the situation.
We have received from them this month a pattern book of Irish Christmas cards, all designed,
printed, and fully finished in their factory at Cork. . . . . Hundreds of thousands of
these should be ordered by the trade for next Christmas, as they are excellent value, and meet
the requirements of all who want a Christmas card at once Irish and cheap in price.”

COINAGE.

The Britizsh sovereign containsg 113 grains
of pure gold (worth 2ith pence per grain)
and should weigh 123.27 grains troy. The
term carat when applied to gold refers to
quality rather than weight. Gold can be
beaten into leaves one two hundredth thou-
sandth part of an inch in thicknegs.

One sovereign (1233 grains) can be made
to spread over 6,500 square feet.

The approximate value of old gold is:—

9 carat 18 worth about £1 11 10 per oz. troy

These are about the rates a jeweller
would pay you. Gold is legal tender to
any amount.

SILVER.
Standard Bilver contains thirty seven
fortieths of fine silver and three fortieths

of alloy. 8ilver stamped with a Lion is
equal in quality to silver coins. A shilling
contains about eighty grains of pure silver
(value about 6d., other coins pro rata).
Silver is a legal tender in sums not exceed-
ing two pounds.

ERONZE.

Bronze is made up of copper 95 parts,
tin 4 parts, zinc one part. Bronze is not
a legal tender for sums exceeding one
shilling. The half-penny is exactly ons
inch in diameter. The penny is exactly
one and one fifth inch in diameter. Three
pennies, or five halfpennieg, or ten farthings
weigh one ounce avoirdupois.
